title: Akakura
description: Shimamura wakes up in Adachi's body. A body-swap story that explores how deeply they understand each other—and how much they still have to learn.
content:
I didn't remember hitting my head on anything—yesterday or any other day—so I had no clue what could've caused this.

I rubbed my eyes, blinked them open, and froze.

My back went stiff. Not because of what I was seeing—more because the air itself felt… off. This wasn't the smell of my room. The room looked familiar, but in a way that made me uneasy.

Then my gaze caught on the boomerang on the shelf.

Ah. Of course. This was Adachi's room. But why was I in Adachi's room? I was sure I'd fallen asleep in my own bed.

I stood up, but even that felt off—like my body wasn't calibrated correctly. Instinctively, I looked down at my hands… and instantly realized they weren't mine. A small wave of panic tightened my chest.

I searched around for a mirror, rumbled through some cosmetics, found a little hand mirror, and lifted it—

'…Huh.'

Adachi's face looked back at me.

'…Well. This is new.'

I moved an arm, then a leg, just to test it. Everything responded, but not in a way that felt natural.

This probably wasn't a dream. So if I was in Adachi's body… was Adachi in mine?

'Adachi—!' I called out without thinking.

Her voice slipped out of my mouth—soft and low—and hearing it from the inside made me blink. So this was how Adachi always heard herself. Weirdly fascinating.

But there was no reply. Not even the faintest stir. And knowing her, there was no way she'd be sleeping peacefully if we were sharing the same body.

That left only one conclusion. This wasn't possession—it was a swap. Which meant Adachi was out there somewhere, wide awake… in my body.

Honestly, that wasn't the worst thing in the world. In more ways than one.

I glanced at her reflection in the mirror and gently poked her cheek.

'…Doesn't feel that cute…'

Saying that about her face felt wrong. Adachi was cute—objectively—but somehow the expressions didn't sit right on me. It looked like her, but without her heart behind it, it didn't feel like Adachi at all.

Then another thought surfaced.

Now that I could use Adachi's voice, I could make her say things she'd never say to me in her life.

That mischievous thought bubbled up hard.

I'd already gotten more than enough compliments from her—so why not try going in the opposite direction?

'Sigh—well… Shimamura, you idiot! Scum! You're not even human!'

The moment the words left my mouth, I froze. It hit far harder than I'd anticipated. I couldn't imagine Adachi ever cursing someone like that. Hearing such venom spill from her soft, gentle voice rattled me. If she truly felt that way, it would probably break me in seconds.

I tried to think of something else.

'Ehhh—Shimamura wants to hang out? Only if she treats me first—'

I tried for a 'mean Adachi,' but even that left a hollow ache in my chest. It felt like I'd glimpsed a version of her that could think like this, and the possibility unsettled me deeply.

No. This wasn't right. I needed to stick to the Adachi I knew.

I am Adachi. How are you?

'I'm fine…'

Moving through the house felt strange. Her body resisted my movements, making each step down the stairs deliberate and heavy.

At the bottom, someone appeared in my path, and I greeted them instinctively.

'Good morning,' It was my habit to greet anyone I ran into at home in the morning, no matter who they were.

Adachi's mother froze, clearly startled.

She stayed expressionless, as if instinctively responding before thinking.

Her voice was sharp, almost scraping the floor, her eyes piercing, and her lips seemed incapable of forming anything pleasant.

Even though I wasn't Adachi, I couldn't help but feel a little intimidated by her presence.

'Good morning.'

'…Yes.'

I slipped away before Adachi's mother could recover. Not being very familiar with the layout of her house, I walked straight down the hallway, eventually turning into the kitchen at the far end.

The clean kitchen felt strangely lifeless. The air was cold, as if silently declaring that it wasn't used often. Though it was roughly the same size as my own kitchen, it had far more empty spaces and carried an unmistakable sense of loneliness. Perhaps my own kitchen was simply too lively.

Unsurprisingly, there was no breakfast laid out on the table.

Adachi's mother, having caught up, made no effort to hide her confusion. She looked at me and asked,

'Are you… eating breakfast today?'

'I, um… think I'll skip it.'

So Adachi really didn't eat here much. Well, better to just get out of here for now. Back on the second floor, I was already starting to feel just how difficult it was to be Adachi.

Things on her side were probably a complete mess too—should I call to check on her?

'…No, never mind. Better not.'

Just when I was thinking of checking Adachi's phone, I realized I didn't know her passcode. She probably couldn't use my phone either… But then a thought flashed through my mind—if it's Adachi, she might just figure out some way to unlock it.

Looks like I have no choice but to meet her face-to-face. If that's the case, I'll change into my uniform and head over to my house right away.

I really hope things like this would only happen on weekends. With a sigh, I grabbed my uniform from the hanger and quickly took off my pajamas. Thankfully, our uniforms are the same style, so changing didn't take long. But once I put on the short skirt, I realized even more just how slim Adachi's waist was and how nice her curves looked.

Even the areas normally hidden by the uniform screamed textbook 'beautiful girl.' There was something about her—and beautiful girls, that seemed effortlessly flawless.

While tying the uniform ribbon, I glanced into the mirror and let 'Adachi' take a good, curious look at herself. Seeing myself turned into such a pretty girl, I couldn't help feeling a bit moved—So this is me, huh… But I didn't feel anything like 'This is my body now! I'm never giving it back!' Not at all. I liked myself more than I expected—and if I became Adachi forever, I wouldn't get to see her reactions anymore. And that's what I wanted most: to witness her joys and worries.

Still, I had no clue what a beauty like her normally did each morning before school.

There were tons of cosmetics, but all I could think was, What even are all these for?—which definitely didn't match the mindset of a high school girl.

When I'd just entered high school I'd had a little enthusiasm for this kind of thing, but the more troublesome it felt, the lazier I got. Lately, though, so I wouldn't embarrass myself around Adachi, I'd at least put on a little makeup—but I still slacked off from time to time. Ahh geez… just thinking that while using Adachi's body made me feel earnestly embarrassed.

Seeing how much she'd been trying, I could feel her affection behind it, and my face grew hot.

Layer after layer of embarrassment washed over me, and I kept blushing as I smiled—a genuine, heartfelt smile.

But it's no good—when I smiled, it didn't look like Adachi at all. I finally understood that expressions are really just the shape of one's heart. My thinking wasn't very clear, but I still reached one conclusion: Adachi is best when she looks like Adachi.

At that moment, I heard soft rustling footsteps behind me and turned around with an 'Ah—'.

As usual, she suddenly appeared behind me, hopping on one foot like she was trying to surprise me.

'Is that you, Shimamura?'

'Ah, so you noticed.'

Seeing Yashiro pop up as casually as a tanuki and casually talk to me, I felt a bit relieved. I grabbed my bag and left the room with her. Imagining Adachi holding onto that girl—something you rarely saw—made me laugh.

'Morning—'

'A good morning to you. Did you do something?'

'Heh heh heh, even though you're always saying I don't do anything and just freeload?'

'True enough.'

So it wasn't her mysterious cosmic powers. Honestly, I'd exhausted every logical explanation I could think of.

'Why did this happen?'—well, I'd just let that question go.

'Whatever. I'm sure by tomorrow everything will go back to normal!'

'Waa—'

With that completely baseless optimism, I decided to head to my house.

'I'm going home for a bit. What about you? Want to come with me?'

'Grandma called me over today, so I'm going there.'

'Ah, definitely a tanuki.'

I let go of her hand and released her back into the wild. With a 'waaa—' she ran deeper into the house.

Before I could even wonder how she planned to leave, she had already vanished—footsteps gone. Probably teleported. At almost the same moment, Adachi's mom walked out of the kitchen and squinted at me.

'Um, I'm heading out.'

Wondering whether Adachi usually greeted her or not, I still offered a greeting, and Adachi's mom gave a stiff nod.

'Be careful… Isn't it a bit early?'

'Ah—uh—mm, ah—ah—uh—'

Before I could slip up, I practically tripped over myself putting on shoes and heading out.

'Hold on… your bike key.'

'Th—thank you—! Thank you so much.'

I took the key from her and finally stepped outside. Right then, as if timing it perfectly, a figure dashed toward the house.

Ah. It was me.

Gasping for breath, I—Adachi—arrived at Adachi's house. She held her knees, lifted her head toward me, and her face stiffened. When I flashed a peace sign, her expression twisted even more.

Hunched over, shorter than usual.

'Um… who might you be?'

Her posture and tone told me everything I needed to know, and I finally relaxed. I didn't exactly love the idea of someone else controlling my body, but if it was Adachi… I could live with it. She'd handle my body more gently—and more carefully—than I ever would.

'Eh? I'm Adachi.'

Once I relaxed, mischief bubbled up. I imitated Adachi's usual somewhat cold tone, and Adachi—well, it was complicated—actually shivered.

'Aren't you… Shimamura?'

It was the first time I realized my face could make such weak, dependent, trembling expressions.

Yep—Adachi really had taken over my body. A funny feeling blossomed inside me.

'I told you, I'm Adachi.'

'……………………………………………………………………………………'

'Sorry, sorry. I just felt like teasing you.'

I laughed, and the 'me' in front of me twisted her expression awkwardly. She probably thought it looked strange to see herself laughing so freely.

'WOW, something crazy really happened.'

'Y-yeah… I'm just… wondering what we should do…'

Since she'd run all the way here, she was still panting, her face completely red—totally like Adachi.

'It'll be fine. Stuff like this will be back to normal tomorrow.'

'R-really?'

'Of course!'

Even though it was my voice, Adachi being so decisive felt strangely off.

'So… should we just go to school?'

I twirled the bike key and suggested it. Adachi looked down and made a tiny 'mm…' sound. She was way too cute. If the one inside was Adachi, I could genuinely fall for myself at this rate.

We brought the bike out. I said I'd ride today and sat down.

'C-can you? Can Shimamura ride?'

'I can. Because this is your body.'

Telling her not to worry, I had her stand behind me. The first push on the pedal felt absurdly heavy.

But after a few turns, the body naturally adjusted, and the wheels spun like they were connected directly to me.

'But waking up like this was a huge shock.'

'Me too.'

'Adachi, did you do anything bad with my body?'

'Pff!'

It was a joke, but she made a sound like she'd been smacked in the face.

I slowed down and looked back. In my body, Adachi was hiccupping and wide-eyed, her face pale—totally flustered.

'Wait… you did do something?'

'………Um… the wind… the wind is too loud… I can't… hear…'

She tapped her ears over and over, insisting she couldn't hear. Sometimes Adachi really was like a force of nature. Judging from her reaction, it wasn't that she used my body to do something—it was that she did something to my body.

Come to think of it, I was wearing the school uniform too. Could she really stay calm while changing clothes in my body?

Just imagining it made my face itch.

'Uh… anyway… breakfast is really important… okay…?'

'…Okay.'

After saying that, we walked in silence all the way to school, keeping perfectly proper the whole time.

When we reached the classroom, we paused to think about our seats. I… well, this was a bit troublesome, I realized, as Adachi tugged at my school uniform.

'What is it?'

'Shi… Ada…chi. That's my seat.'

'Oh, right.'

Of course the seats were swapped too. I apologized and walked to Adachi's seat.

Sitting down felt unstable, like one chair leg was shorter. My posture and sense of balance were off. Maybe because it wasn't my usual seat, but I realized another reason.

I was hungry. This body that hadn't eaten breakfast was crying out. Adachi probably didn't register this kind of hunger as pain. I suddenly thought: Adachi must've grown used to all sorts of pain.

Class started. I got my notebook ready, but something felt off. As I copied the notes, my handwriting came out crooked. Then I realized—I was using my left hand. Adachi was left-handed.

The muscle memory was there, yet my mind and body weren't in sync. Even though my left hand moved smoothly, the sensation was strangely unsettling.

Even though Adachi's seat was closer to the board, the writings on the board felt harder to see.

I never had this problem from my own seat, which made me realize one of the few areas I was actually better than Adachi in. Meanwhile, Adachi was making full use of my clearer eyesight… to stare at me instead of the board. I could feel her gaze from the back.

Well, whatever she was seeing, I'm Adachi on the outside anyway.

I never thought I'd appreciate a class where we couldn't move around so much. The less we moved, the fewer mistakes we made.

I managed to maintain the facade of 'Adachi' until the end of the day.

Thankfully, Adachi didn't have work today. We couldn't go on a date like this anyway, though honestly I kind of wanted to try. In the end, we just went home separately.

'If we don't turn back tomorrow, w-what do we do…'

'Then we'll just do our best to live!'

My over-optimistic non-comfort left Adachi speechless, and then we walked home.

Even though I took a little time figuring out the route, I made it to Adachi's house before sunset.

'I'm home—…'

I opened the door cautiously and called out, but indoors there was only dim light and quiet air. Her mom must still be at work. I turned on the entryway light and headed upstairs.

Back in the room, after changing and pacing a bit, I sat on the bed hugging my knees.

I'd been too panicked in the morning to notice.

'There's almost nothing to do in this room.'

No novels or manga on the shelf. There was a TV, but no remote in sight. At my house, I had my little sister, and I could always capture the rare upright animal wandering around the house to kill time. Thinking of such ordinary everyday scenes made this quiet, knee-hugging moment feel strangely precious instead.

How did Adachi spend her days in this room?

In this deafening silence, the answer was obvious. She must have been thinking about me the whole time, waiting for my calls. I couldn't help thinking that once things went back to normal, I should take the initiative to call her more often.

Imagining Adachi sitting still waiting for my call, I pressed my big toes together thoughtfully.

'Hmm—…'

I jumped off the bed, now used to the body. Picked up pen and paper and headed to the kitchen.

There was no reason to rush, but time was passing too slowly—slow enough to make me want to fight it.

In the kitchen, I wrote a note for Adachi's mom:

'I want breakfast tomorrow. Please.'

It might be overstepping, but for Adachi's health, I couldn't help but insist a little.

When I finished and lifted my head, I realized just how quiet the house was.

The house was quiet. Frozen. Nothing moved. Nothing connected. If every day were like this, I'd feel lonely too.

But maybe what Adachi felt was different.

Yes—Adachi and I were different. Not quite in sync. Different, yet bound to each other.

So—Climbing the stairs, thinking about the long hours I'd spend alone until morning, I prayed quietly:

'Let me see Adachi tomorrow.'

And the next morning, for some reason, we were back to normal.

Great. With the joy of returning to myself, I ran down the hallway and peeked into the kitchen. My mom was sitting as usual, but her toes were tapping with irritation. Still staring at the wall, she turned toward me.

After confirming my face, she let out a deep—very deep—sigh.

'Good morning—'

I checked her up and down to make sure I had the right person before greeting her.

But my mother hesitated clearly, wavered, and finally—

'…Good morning.'

A hard, floor-scraping kind of voice; a sharp gaze like she was glaring at something; and lips that could never say anything kind.

The exact mother I'd seen yesterday reappeared.

Ah. I immediately understood: those two had swapped now.

'Mom, thanks for the lunch!'

'…Ha?'

It was Adachi's mom.

But since my real mom's behavior was already weird, no one noticed—and just like that, we got through another swapped day.
